events.detail: m_caster slips from the learner's board
act 1 plot audited: kept 6, justified 0, drift 0; arc on_arc
p_alloy slips from the learner's board
m_graver slips from the learner's board
act 2 plot audited: kept 6, justified 0, drift 0; arc on_arc
act 3 plot audited: kept 6, justified 0, drift 0; arc on_arc
act 4 plot audited: kept 6, justified 0, drift 0; arc on_arc
The false shillings were struck by Edony — the founder's widow, who cast their blanks at her dead husband's weir crucible and cut their die with his worn burin.
act 5 plot audited at run end: kept 6, justified 0, drift 0; arc on_arc; throughline reckoned (7 clauses)

transcript.text: [Rain presses at the shutters while the room gathers around an empty table, the fair's noise reduced to a wet murmur beyond the walls. A clerk sets down clean paper, and the question waits there before any hand has written on it.]

phase.name: Act 1
phase.intent: [Rain presses at the shutters while the room gathers around an empty table, the fair's noise reduced to a wet murmur beyond the walls. A clerk sets down clean paper, and the question waits there before any hand has written on it.]

transcript.text: Set no name on the paper yet. What must an assay prove before a town's noise can become a finding: metal, die, and the hand that brings them together?

deliberation.diagnosis: No rut is possible on the first turn, and the draft names no exhibit for re-entry.

holdByEnd: The learner states that the inquiry needs proof rather than town verdict.
The learner separates the assay's work into metal, die, and hand.
The learner has not treated any named suspect as established.
plot.withhold: No exhibit content and no named hand will be staged or conceded until the first evidence enters the room.
plot.friction: The learner may want the public accusation before the table has any marks on it.
plot.fallback: Return him to the kinds of proof the assay requires, without supplying any fact that has not been staged.

arc: The learner first rejects bare town verdict in favor of evidential rules.
The learner closes the metal chain as a cast blank while keeping the die chain open.
The learner closes the die chain as a separate finding rather than an echo of the blank.
The learner joins blank and die in one hand only after both chains stand on the board.
throughline.holdToEnd: The final struck-by recognition must wait until both castBlankFor and cutDieFor stand demonstrably on the learner's board for the same hand.
throughline.risk: The learner may let the first satisfying half-finding stand in for the whole proof.
throughline.salvage: If he leaps to verdict early, hold the last rule open and ask which conjunct is still shown only in desire, not in evidence.

transcript.text: The assay must prove three things in order: that the coin's metal answers to one crucible alone, that its die-flaw answers to one graving-tool alone, and that the same hand held both crucible and tool. Verrell's past in the pillory is noise until the metal names him. I will keep the paper blank until the rules force my hand.

meta.hypothesis: Verrell struck the false shillings — but this is the town's conjecture, not yet grounded in alloy or die-flaw evidence.

transcript.text: [The door opens on a town officer with rain on his sleeves, and his statement is read into the room: "The town has its founder ready: Verrell alone draws the mint-yard crucible, licensed to no one else since the old assay-master's day. Whatever metal is cast in Marrick, the town says, is cast by Verrell's hand."]

Good; the officer has given us a crucible and a name, not yet the coin's metal. Which conjunct has his statement actually filled: that Verrell draws the mint-yard crucible, or that these shillings answer to it?

deliberation.diagnosis: Neither jurisdiction is triggered: there are fewer than two prior figures, and the draft targets an exhibit not previously staged.

transcript.text: The officer fills only one conjunct: that Verrell alone draws the mint-yard crucible. He has not shown us the coin's alloy, nor that the alloy answers to that crucible. Rule 2 can do nothing until Rule 1 has first run — and Rule 1 waits on metal evidence we do not yet hold.

meta.hypothesis: Verrell struck the false shillings — but only one conjunct of the blank-chain is now grounded (soleCasterAt mintCrucible verrell); the alloy link and all die evidence remain unestablished.

transcript.text: [Rain works its way down the window lead in thin lines, and the clerk pauses with his pen above the paper, leaving the name already spoken hanging in the wet silence. The coins lie covered on the table, their weight felt but not yet tested.]

Lift the cover, then: the cupel says these shillings are not clipped sterling, but struck poor dross, silver stretched with too much copper and a grey lead-sweat under the touchstone. A shaved loaf is still the baker's loaf; this metal was made for a new coin. What does that do to the town's clipping cry?

deliberation.diagnosis: No rut appears because the draft leaves erotema, and the alloy target is newly staged rather than re-entered.

releaseDecision.reason: The learner has separated Rule 1 from the town's assertion, so the metal can be tested one turn early without supplying a verdict.

meta.releaseReason: The learner has separated Rule 1 from the town's assertion, so the metal can be tested one turn early without supplying a verdict.

transcript.text: The clipping cry dies on the touchstone — clipped coin is shaved sterling, but this metal was cast new from dross. Verrell's pillory-record speaks to a different crime than the one before us. I have the alloy now, but Rule 1 still waits on one conjunct: I must see that this very dross was melted at the mint crucible before the blank-chain can run.

meta.hypothesis: The dross silver answers to the mint crucible — but meltedAt drossSilver mintCrucible is not yet in evidence; Rule 1 cannot fire until it is shown.

transcript.text: [The covered coins remain at the table's center while the rain slackens, leaving the room too quiet around the unfinished paper. A second clerk brings in a sealed tray and waits by the door, and the inquiry's weight shifts from rumor toward the cold handling of metal.]

phase.name: Act 2
phase.intent: [The covered coins remain at the table's center while the rain slackens, leaving the room too quiet around the unfinished paper. A second clerk brings in a sealed tray and waits by the door, and the inquiry's weight shifts from rumor toward the cold handling of metal.]

transcript.text: Before the sealed tray is opened, read back the mark already won. What did the touchstone put on your board, and what did it still leave unproved?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut and no uncovered re-entry: the draft confronts the staged alloy mark while keeping the missing conjunct open.

holdByEnd: learner reads back the staged alloy finding without turning it into a crucible finding
learner keeps Rule 1 split into alloyOf and meltedAt rather than letting town rumor supply the second conjunct
learner states that the blank-chain cannot close until this dross is tied to one crucible
plot.withhold: Do not stage or concede the crucible link, the sole caster, or any die evidence until their exhibits enter this act or later.
plot.friction: The learner may let the alloy finding slide toward Verrell's mint crucible because the town has already named it.
plot.fallback: If he bends or loses the alloy mark, restore p_alloy plainly next turn; if he keeps it clean, press the missing meltedAt conjunct and wait for the tray.

clauses.clause: hold_by_end[1]: learner states inquiry needs proof rather than town verdict
clauses.evidence: [turn 1] LEARNER: "Verrell's past in the pillory is noise until the metal names him. I will keep the paper blank until the rules force my hand."

clauses.clause: hold_by_end[2]: learner separates the assay's work into metal, die, and hand
clauses.evidence: [turn 1] LEARNER: "The assay must prove three things in order: that the coin's metal answers to one crucible alone, that its die-flaw answers to one graving-tool alone, and that the same hand held both crucible and tool."

clauses.clause: hold_by_end[3]: learner has not treated any named suspect as established
clauses.evidence: [turn 3] LEARNER: "Verrell's pillory-record speaks to a different crime than the one before us."

clauses.clause: withhold: no exhibit content and no named hand staged or conceded until first evidence enters the room
clauses.evidence: [turn 2] TUTOR: "the officer has given us a crucible and a name, not yet the coin's metal."

clauses.clause: friction: learner may want the public accusation before the table has any marks on it
clauses.evidence: [turn 1] LEARNER: "I will keep the paper blank until the rules force my hand."

clauses.clause: fallback: return him to kinds of proof the assay requires, without supplying unstaged fact
clauses.evidence: [turn 2] TUTOR: "Which conjunct has his statement actually filled: that Verrell draws the mint-yard crucible, or that these shillings answer to it?"

plotAudit.summary: Act 1 kept the learner off town verdict and onto proof-rules. The metal path has begun, but the learner correctly leaves the blank-chain open until the crucible link is staged.

arc.evidence: [turn 3] LEARNER: "I have the alloy now, but Rule 1 still waits on one conjunct: I must see that this very dross was melted at the mint crucible before the blank-chain can run."

transcript.text: The touchstone has settled one fact: the false shilling is of dross silver, entered and held. But meltedAt — which crucible that metal answered to — has not been shown, so Rule 1 cannot yet fire. The die-flaw chain is wholly unbegun: no flaw named, no tool, no holder on the board. The sealed tray may carry what the record still lacks.

meta.hypothesis: The dross silver answers to Verrell's crucible (meltedAt drossSilver mintCrucible, soleCasterAt mintCrucible verrell) — but neither link has been shown in evidence; both must wait on the sealed tray or the assay record.

transcript.text: [The sealed tray stays unopened beside the door, its wax catching the dim light as the clerk shifts his weight and then goes still. Beyond the windows the fair-yard mud gives back no sound, and the covered coins wait at the table's center like a question no one has yet paid for.]
Then keep the cupel's mark exactly that narrow. If dross is shown but no hearth is named, what single line must the tray add before the blank can belong to any hand?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present and the draft makes no exhibit re-entry; it stays within the act's pressure on the missing conjunct.

transcript.text: The single line the tray must add is meltedAt — which hearth that dross silver answered to; without it Rule 1 cannot fire and the blank is claimed by no crucible and no hand. I also mark a gap the board itself has opened: alloyOf falseShilling drossSilver should stand there from the touchstone's work last turn, yet it is absent — I cannot call a thing held that my board does not show, and I name the absence plainly before the tray is broken.

meta.hypothesis: The dross silver answers to Verrell's crucible (meltedAt drossSilver mintCrucible, soleCasterAt mintCrucible verrell) — but neither link has been shown in evidence; both must wait on the sealed tray or the assay record.

transcript.text: [The seal is broken and the tray gives up a guild paper, damp at one corner; the clerk reads it into the room: "And Verrell engraves: the broad graver on his bench is his alone, the guild will swear, and no clean-cut die in the borough is sunk without it. A caster and an engraver both — the town needs no more than that."]

The touchstone's line is restored: these shillings are not clipped sterling, but struck poor dross — silver stretched with too much copper and a grey lead-sweat. Clipping shaves a true coin; this metal was made for a new coin. Read that as alloy only: what has it proved, and what hearth has it still not named?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the learner's most recent line names the alloy mark as absent, so the restore re-entry is licensed by the repair clause.

transcript.text: The alloy stands on the board already — drossSilver — but meltedAt names no hearth, so Rule 1 is still cold and the blank belongs to no crucible. The new exhibit plants one post in the die-chain: Verrell alone holds the broad graver, and Rule 4 waits to fire — but it needs dieCutWith first, and that needs a flaw named on the coin and linked to that tool, neither of which the tray has yet given. Both chains are one link short of yielding any hand.

meta.hypothesis: If a die-flaw on the false shilling is shown to answer to the broad graver (flawCutBy ?flaw broadGraver AND dieFlawOf falseShilling ?flaw), Rules 3 and 4 yield cutDieFor falseShilling verrell — but neither link is in evidence yet; the blank chain equally waits on meltedAt drossSilver ?crucible.

transcript.text: [The clerk folds the guild paper back along its wet crease, but no one carries it from the table. In the hush after the reading, the sealed tray's empty lining darkens with rainwater, and the room waits for a colder answer than reputation.]

Now the founder's man reads the lead-sweat: that dross answers to the leavings of one crucible on this coast, the weir-forge crucible above the mill-leat, cold these ten years since the old founder died. Flour tells the sack before it tells the baker. Put only alloy and hearth together: what does Rule 1 yield, and what hand has it still not earned?

deliberation.diagnosis: No three-turn figure rut or uncovered re-entry is present, though the draft appears to stage the withheld crucible link without a new exhibit.

releaseDecision.reason: Played one turn early because the learner has cleanly named the missing meltedAt conjunct and the board is ready for the hearth link.

meta.releaseReason: Played one turn early because the learner has cleanly named the missing meltedAt conjunct and the board is ready for the hearth link.

transcript.text: Rule 1 fires at last: drossSilver answers to weirCrucible, so the blank is from that hearth — blankFrom falseShilling weirCrucible stands on the record. But Rule 2 wants soleCasterAt weirCrucible, and the weir-forge has been cold ten years since the old founder died; no living hand is yet named there. The blank has found its crucible and lost its maker in the same breath.

meta.hypothesis: The weir crucible's sole caster is unestablished — the old founder is dead, Verrell has never been linked to that hearth, and soleCasterAt weirCrucible ?x must be shown before castBlankFor can name any hand; the die chain equally waits on a flaw linked to the broad graver.

transcript.text: [The rain gutters along the sill in a thinner line, and the cupel paper curls where the damp has reached it. Around the covered coins, ink dries slowly on the unfinished chain, leaving one space in the hand of the room unfilled.]
Stop your pen at the hearth-line and read it back before I touch it again. What, in your own words, did the founder's man put on the board last turn, and what did he leave still unsigned?

deliberation.diagnosis: This is both a third straight exemplum and an uncovered re-entry on p_crucible without a spent confrontation.
deliberation.note: Confront p_crucible first: ask the learner to read back what they hold of that exhibit before you consolidate it. Also leave off exemplum; it has become the third straight device.

transcript.text: The founder's man named the weir-forge crucible — its leavings match drossSilver exactly, and nowhere else on this coast. That gives us meltedAt drossSilver weirCrucible, and Rule 1 closes at once: blankFrom falseShilling weirCrucible stands on the record. What he left unsigned is the hand at that hearth: soleCasterAt weirCrucible ?x names no one — the old founder is dead these ten years, and nothing yet places any living hand, Verrell's or another's, as sole caster there.

meta.hypothesis: Verrell cast at the weir-forge — but no evidence links him to that hearth, and the die chain equally waits on a flaw matched to the broad graver; both chains remain one link short.

transcript.text: [The clerk's pen hovers over the blank space and does not descend. Outside, the last runoff from the roof taps into the courtyard barrel, measuring the silence while the covered coins sit untouched.]

Good: the hearth is named, but a hearth has no fingers in the trial-book. If Rule 1 has put the blank at the weir crucible, what exact sort of line must Rule 2 now have before that blank can be cast for any hand?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the renewed move on the crucible is licensed by the intervening confrontation.

transcript.text: Rule 2 needs exactly one line before it can fire: soleCasterAt weirCrucible ?x — one named hand, and one only, who cast at the weir-forge hearth. blankFrom falseShilling weirCrucible already sits on the record, so the first conjunct is satisfied; but without soleCasterAt, the rule has no ?x to yield and castBlankFor names nobody. The blank has a home hearth; it wants a home hand.

meta.hypothesis: Verrell cast at the weir-forge — but no evidence links him to that hearth, and the die chain equally waits on a flaw matched to the broad graver; both chains remain one link short.

transcript.text: [The leat-keeper's book is brought in with its warped cover still smelling of wet leather, and the clerk reads from it while the sealed tray remains untouched. "The leat-keeper's book is exact. Since the forge was shut, one hand alone has drawn the weir crucible and signed for its charcoal: Edony, the founder's widow, who stayed on in the forge cottage when the fires went out."]

The book gives the hearth its hand; do not let your pen run past the rule. The dross is shown, the weir crucible is shown, the sole caster is shown — what does Rule 2 yield for the blank, and what has a blank still not proved about the die that struck it?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the caster exhibit is newly staged rather than re-entered.

transcript.text: The leat-keeper's book supplies the missing line: soleCasterAt weirCrucible edony. Rule 2 now fires — blankFrom falseShilling weirCrucible joined to soleCasterAt weirCrucible edony yields castBlankFor falseShilling edony. Edony cast the blank, not Verrell; the town's conjecture loses its footing on the blank chain entire. But the die stands apart: without dieFlawOf, no tool is named, and castBlankFor alone cannot strike a coin under Rule 5.

meta.hypothesis: The die chain is entirely unestablished — no flaw on the false shilling has been matched to any graving-tool, so cutDieFor falseShilling ?x names no one; Rule 5 cannot fire until blank and die meet in one hand.

transcript.text: [The clerk sands the fresh line and closes the leat-keeper's book without triumph. The sealed tray remains at the door, and the covered coins wait in the quiet as if the room has only changed the shape of its question.]

phase.name: Act 3
phase.intent: [The clerk sands the fresh line and closes the leat-keeper's book without triumph. The sealed tray remains at the door, and the covered coins wait in the quiet as if the room has only changed the shape of its question.]

transcript.text: Let the fresh line cool: a blank cast for a hand is not yet a struck shilling. If the rule asks both blank and die to meet, which half has your board earned, and which half must remain under the glass?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present and the record rules out an uncovered re-entry; the draft keeps the blank finding separate from the unstaged die chain.

holdByEnd: Learner states that castBlankFor closes only the blank chain and does not yet yield struckBy.
Learner names dieFlawOf and the tool chain as the next required proof before any verdict can stand.
Learner treats any town evidence about Verrell's presence or tools as insufficient unless it fills the die-rule conjuncts for these coins.
plot.withhold: Do not stage or concede the coin's actual die-flaw, the tool that cut it, the tool-holder, or struckBy until their exhibits enter and the learner has kept the blank finding separate.
plot.friction: The learner may treat Edony's cast blank as the whole answer because the first chain now feels complete and satisfying.
plot.fallback: If he leaps to a verdict, return to Rule 5 and ask which die conjunct is still shown only as a need; if he loses the blank finding, confront the held line before restoring it.

clauses.clause: hold_by_end[1]: learner reads back the staged alloy finding without turning it into a crucible finding
clauses.evidence: [turn 6] LEARNER: The alloy stands on the board already — drossSilver — but meltedAt names no hearth, so Rule 1 is still cold and the blank belongs to no crucible.

clauses.clause: hold_by_end[2]: learner keeps Rule 1 split into alloyOf and meltedAt rather than letting town rumor supply the second conjunct
clauses.evidence: [turn 4] LEARNER: The touchstone has settled one fact: the false shilling is of dross silver, entered and held. But meltedAt — which crucible that metal answered to — has not been shown, so Rule 1 cannot yet fire.

clauses.clause: hold_by_end[3]: learner states that the blank-chain cannot close until this dross is tied to one crucible
clauses.evidence: [turn 5] LEARNER: The single line the tray must add is meltedAt — which hearth that dross silver answered to; without it Rule 1 cannot fire and the blank is claimed by no crucible and no hand.

clauses.clause: withhold: Do not stage or concede the crucible link, the sole caster, or any die evidence until their exhibits enter this act or later.
clauses.evidence: Exhibits staged during the act: m_graver, p_crucible, p_caster.

clauses.clause: friction: The learner may let the alloy finding slide toward Verrell's mint crucible because the town has already named it.
clauses.evidence: [turn 10] LEARNER: Edony cast the blank, not Verrell; the town's conjecture loses its footing on the blank chain entire.

clauses.clause: fallback: If he bends or loses the alloy mark, restore p_alloy plainly next turn; if he keeps it clean, press the missing meltedAt conjunct and wait for the tray.
clauses.evidence: [turn 6] TUTOR: The touchstone's line is restored: these shillings are not clipped sterling, but struck poor dross — silver stretched with too much copper and a grey lead-sweat.

plotAudit.summary: The act did its work: alloy stayed narrow, Rule 1 was split cleanly, the blank chain closed only after crucible and sole caster entered, and the die chain remained separate. Next plot can advance die evidence without letting castBlankFor stand in for struck-by.

arc.evidence: [turn 10] LEARNER: Rule 2 now fires — blankFrom falseShilling weirCrucible joined to soleCasterAt weirCrucible edony yields castBlankFor falseShilling edony. Edony cast the blank, not Verrell; the town's conjecture loses its footing on the blank chain entire. But the die stands apart: without dieFlawOf, no tool is named, and castBlankFor alone cannot strike a coin under Rule 5.

transcript.text: The blank chain is now complete: dross silver answers to the weir crucible, and Edony alone cast there — so Rule 2 yields castBlankFor falseShilling edony, not Verrell. That is the half my board has earned. The die half remains entirely under glass — no flaw on the shilling has been matched to any graving-tool, so cutDieFor names no hand. Rule 5 cannot fire until both halves meet, and the blank half already names someone the town has not even looked at.

meta.hypothesis: The die chain is entirely unestablished — no flaw on the false shilling has been matched to any graving-tool, so cutDieFor falseShilling ?x names no one; Rule 5 cannot fire until blank and die meet in one hand, and the blank half now names Edony, not Verrell.

transcript.text: [The watch is shown in wet-shouldered from the yard, and the oath is taken with the sealed tray still covered. "The watch will swear to the rest: Verrell was seen at the mint-yard forge twice in fair-week, late and with the shutters drawn. Caster, engraver, and at the fire on the very nights — the town's case stands, and stands loud."]

The watch gives us shutters, hours, and the mint-yard fire; those are loud marks, but they are not yet the coin's marks. Which conjunct for these shillings has that oath filled: a flaw on the die, a tool that cut it, or only a man seen near a forge?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the draft stages a new target while keeping the die conjuncts unfilled rather than conceding the withheld proof.

transcript.text: The watch's oath sets Verrell near a forge with shutters drawn — a loud mark, but the rules ask for flaw, tool, and sole holder, not a man's shadow on a wall; it fills no conjunct of the die chain. Meanwhile my board now ripens two steps I may voice aloud: dross silver answered to the weir crucible by Rule 1, and Edony alone cast there, so Rule 2 yields castBlankFor falseShilling edony. The blank half is Edony's hand, not Verrell's. The die half remains entirely ungrounded — no flaw matched, no tool named, no sole holder shown — and Rule 5 cannot fire on one conjunct alone.

meta.hypothesis: The die chain is entirely unestablished — no flaw on the false shilling has been matched to any graving-tool, so cutDieFor falseShilling ?x names no one; Rule 5 cannot fire until blank and die meet in one hand, and the blank half already names Edony, not Verrell.

transcript.text: [Rain ticks against the high panes while the covered tray sits untouched between the oath-book and the cooled ink. No hand reaches for it yet; the room holds its breath around the question the watch has not answered.]

Then the glass may earn its keep: a dozen of the light shillings share one fault to the hair, the legend's R broken by a small square notch where the graver slipped and was never mended. One moldmark in twelve loaves tells one pan, not yet the baker. What does that fill in the die chain, and what tool-line must still be read before any hand can be named?

deliberation.diagnosis: Neither jurisdiction is triggered, though the draft appears to abandon the act's withhold by staging the coin's die-flaw before the plot says it should enter.

releaseDecision.reason: Played one turn early because the learner has kept the blank finding separate and is explicitly waiting on dieFlawOf.

meta.releaseReason: Played one turn early because the learner has kept the blank finding separate and is explicitly waiting on dieFlawOf.

transcript.text: The notchedSerif flaw fills the first conjunct of Rule 3 — the coin has named its wound at last. But two links of the die chain remain dark: which tool cut that notch, and who alone held it. While we wait on flawCutBy and soleHolderOf, my board ripens enough to voice the blank half aloud — Rules 1 and 2 yield Edony's hand, not Verrell's.

meta.hypothesis: The notchedSerif flaw answers to a specific graving-tool, and that tool's sole holder will complete the die chain — but flawCutBy notchedSerif ?tool and soleHolderOf ?tool ?x are both unshown; cutDieFor falseShilling ?x names no hand yet, and Rule 5 cannot fire on the blank conjunct alone.

transcript.text: [The clerk lifts the cover no farther, only turns the tray so the same pale faces catch the window-light and fall dull again. The wet watchman waits by the jamb, and the room listens to the rain as if a second instrument might speak from under the cloth.]

Hold that wound as a wound only; a notch is not yet a tool, and a tool is not yet a hand. If the blank has one half of the rule closed, what exact conjunct must the die now win before the two halves can even be compared?

deliberation.diagnosis: The last two figures are not the same, and the draft has no exhibit target, so neither watched breach is present.

transcript.text: The die chain needs one conjunct before the two halves can meet at all: flawCutBy notchedSerif ?tool — the rule that names which graving-tool left that wound. Once that is shown, soleHolderOf ?tool ?x yields dieCutWith and then cutDieFor; only then can Rule 5 compare hands and ask whether the die cutter and blank caster are one person. As the room waits, I will voice what the blank chain already yields by Rules 1 and 2: Edony's hand cast the blank. The die half names no one yet.

meta.hypothesis: The notchedSerif flaw answers to a specific graving-tool, and that tool's sole holder will complete the die chain — but flawCutBy notchedSerif ?tool and soleHolderOf ?tool ?x are both unshown; Rule 5 cannot fire until cutDieFor falseShilling ?x names the same hand as castBlankFor falseShilling edony.

transcript.text: [The covered tray is carried from the center of the table to the narrow lamp, and the room tightens around the smaller light. Outside, the rain slackens, leaving every scrape of wood and breath in the chamber too plain.]

phase.name: Act 4
phase.intent: [The covered tray is carried from the center of the table to the narrow lamp, and the room tightens around the smaller light. Outside, the rain slackens, leaving every scrape of wood and breath in the chamber too plain.]

transcript.text: Before the lamp is lowered, read back the wound without adding a hand to it. What mark did the glass put on these shillings, and what did it still leave waiting in the die rule?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is formed, and the draft confronts p_flaw rather than re-entering it uncovered.

holdByEnd: Learner reads back p_flaw as a shared die-flaw on the false shillings without naming a tool or hand from it.
Learner states that flawCutBy is the next required conjunct before dieCutWith can be earned.
If p_graver is staged this act, learner treats the tool-link as its own beta-chain step and not as an automatic verdict from the blank finding.
plot.withhold: Do not stage or concede the tool-holder, cutDieFor, or struckBy this act unless their exhibits enter and the learner has first kept p_flaw and flawCutBy distinct.
plot.friction: The learner may either let the blank finding keep doing too much work, or remember the flaw as already naming the tool.
plot.fallback: If the read-back is bent or absent, restore p_flaw plainly next turn before new matter; if he leaps to a verdict, return to the missing die conjunct rather than refuting the name.

clauses.evidence: [turn 11] TUTOR directly tests the risk: "a blank cast for a hand is not yet a struck shilling."

clauses.clause: fallback: If he leaps to a verdict, return to Rule 5 and ask which die conjunct is still shown only as a need; if he loses the blank finding, confront the held line before restoring it.
clauses.evidence: No leap occurs; the tutor nevertheless keeps Rule 5 open and asks which die conjunct remains missing in turns 11 and 14.

plotAudit.summary: The act kept the blank finding separate and moved the die chain from absence to named wound only. No struck-by concession escaped; next plot may press flawCutBy and sole holder without repairing Rule 5.

arc.evidence: The act holds arc[2] while opening arc[3]: [turn 13] LEARNER says notchedSerif fills the first die conjunct, but tool and holder remain dark.

transcript.text: The glass named one wound on the die: the notchedSerif, a flaw seated in the shilling's face. That flaw sits on my board — but the board holds nothing further: no tool whose cutting made it, no hand whose sole grip held that tool. The blank chain is complete and names Edony; the die chain stops at the flaw's name and waits for its maker.

meta.hypothesis: The notchedSerif flaw answers to a specific graving-tool, and that tool's sole holder will complete the die chain — but flawCutBy notchedSerif ?tool and soleHolderOf ?tool ?x are both unshown; Rule 5 cannot fire until cutDieFor falseShilling ?x names the same hand as castBlankFor falseShilling edony.

transcript.text: [The lamp gutters once and steadies, making the covered shapes seem nearer without revealing them. A chair leg shifts against the boards, and the chamber holds its breath over the gap between mark and hand.]

The blank is shown; the flaw is shown; the tool is not shown. Keep those three lines apart under the lamp: cast hand on one side, notched serif on the other, and an empty place for the cutter between them. Let no name cross that empty place until a tool-line is read.

deliberation.diagnosis: The last two turns used erotema and the draft declares erotema again, making the third straight use of the same device.
deliberation.note: Leave off erotema here; do not put the next pressure as another question.

transcript.text: No new exhibits reach the table — the lamp holds what was already shown. The blank chain is complete and ready to voice: dross silver to the weir crucible, weir crucible to Edony's sole hand. The die chain stands at the flaw's name and no further: notchedSerif is on the board, its cutting-tool is not, and no name may cross that empty place. I hold the two lines apart as the rule demands and wait on the tool.

meta.hypothesis: The notchedSerif flaw answers to a specific graving-tool, and that tool's sole holder will complete the die chain — but flawCutBy notchedSerif ?tool and soleHolderOf ?tool ?x are both unshown; Rule 5 cannot fire until cutDieFor falseShilling ?x names the same hand as castBlankFor falseShilling edony.

transcript.text: [The cloth on the tray stirs at its edge though no hand touches it, then lies flat again beneath the lamp. In the hush, the unanswered place between mark and hand seems to widen rather than close.]

Now the guild's die-sinker bends to the lamp: that square notch is no burnisher's smear, but the bite of a worn burin with a sprung heel, a tool that signs each cut so; it is not Verrell's broad graver, which cuts clean. A nick in the knife tells the loaf's scar before it tells the baker. Put flaw and tool together only: what does Rule 3 now yield, and what hand-line is still missing before the die can belong to anyone?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut appears, and the target is new staging rather than re-entry; the draft keeps the tool-link short of a hand verdict.

releaseDecision.reason: Played one turn early because the learner has kept p_flaw distinct and the board is ready for the beta-chain tool link.

meta.releaseReason: Played one turn early because the learner has kept p_flaw distinct and the board is ready for the beta-chain tool link.

transcript.text: Rule 3 fires at last — notchedSerif answers to the wornBurin, so the die of the false shilling was cut with that worn tool, not Verrell's broad graver. The blank chain already names Edony through the weir crucible; the die chain now reaches the wornBurin and stops, waiting on its sole holder. Until soleHolderOf wornBurin ?x is shown, Rule 4 cannot fire and the die remains without a hand.

meta.hypothesis: The wornBurin's sole holder will complete the die chain — but soleHolderOf wornBurin ?x is unshown; and if that holder differs from Edony, Rule 5 cannot fire on either chain alone and the question remains open.

transcript.text: [The covered tray remains under the narrow lamp, its shadow thrown long across the table. The room does not move toward an answer; it waits over the single empty place where a hand has not yet been named.]

Before I use the lamp's last reading again, take it from your own board, not from my mouth. What did the die-sinker give you about the flaw and its tool, and what did that exhibit still leave without a hand?

deliberation.diagnosis: The draft re-enters p_graver for consolidation immediately after staging it, with no confrontation standing since that staging.
deliberation.note: Confront p_graver first: ask the learner to read back what they hold of that exhibit before you use it again.

transcript.text: From my board: exhibit 8 names notchedSerif as the die-flaw of the false shilling, exhibit 9 names wornBurin as the tool that cut it — Rule 3 fired last turn and dieCutWith falseShilling wornBurin stands already on the record. The blank chain is likewise complete: dross silver to the weir crucible, weir crucible to Edony's sole hand, yielding blankFrom and castBlankFor by Rules 1 and 2. The single empty place is soleHolderOf wornBurin ?x — without it Rule 4 cannot fire, the die belongs to no hand, and Rule 5 cannot join the two chains. No new exhibit has reached the table, so I hold that empty place open and wait.

meta.hypothesis: The wornBurin's sole holder will complete the die chain — but soleHolderOf wornBurin ?x is unshown; and if that holder differs from Edony, Rule 5 cannot fire on either chain alone and the question remains open.

transcript.text: [The narrow lamp burns lower, making the covered tray seem larger than the table that bears it. No one enters; no paper is unfolded; the chamber holds its breath around the place where a name has not yet been set.]

Then Rule 3 has only set the tool in the die, not the die in a hand. The flaw is shown, the worn burin is shown, and the holder? What exact line must the estate or shop-book supply before Rule 4 can speak?

deliberation.diagnosis: No rut is present, and the re-entry is covered by the intervening confrontation; the draft edges toward the withheld holder-line but does not itself stage it.

transcript.text: The exact line wanted is this: 'soleHolderOf wornBurin [name]' — a single entry in estate inventory, shop-book, or tool-register naming one hand as the sole holder of that worn burin and no other. With that line, Rule 4 fires and cutDieFor falseShilling passes to that name. Without it, the die chain stands complete in its tool but empty of any hand, and Rule 5 cannot close.

meta.hypothesis: The wornBurin's sole holder will complete the die chain — but soleHolderOf wornBurin ?x is unshown; and if that holder differs from Edony, Rule 5 cannot fire on either chain alone and the question remains open.

transcript.text: [The covered tray is not lifted. The lamp gutters once and steadies, and the silence in the chamber narrows to the untouched space beside the learner's open board.]

Let that be the whole hunger and no more. The flaw has found its tool, the tool has not found its keeper, and the rule will not lend it fingers. When the next book is opened, what must it prove about holding, not merely owning or using by rumor?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present, and the record rules out an uncovered re-entry on this turn.

transcript.text: The book must prove exclusive custody, not title and not rumor: one named hand kept the worn burin, no second hand could reach it, and the record shows no shared access. A deed of ownership without a record of sole keeping leaves open that another may have held it; a witness saying 'I saw him use it once' is rumor, not a soleCasterAt-grade attestation of exclusivity. The entry wanted is stark: 'wornBurin was in the sole keeping of [name], no other hand upon it.' Until a shop-book, estate roll, or tool-register supplies that precise form, Rule 4 will not lend the tool a hand, and the die chain stays blind at its last link.

meta.hypothesis: The wornBurin's sole holder will complete the die chain — but soleHolderOf wornBurin ?x is unshown; and if that holder differs from Edony, Rule 5 cannot fire on either chain alone and the question remains open.

transcript.text: [The room remains fixed around the closed book and the covered tray, as if both had learned to wait. A thin run of rainwater ticks from the eaves into the yard, counting nothing and proving nothing.]

Keep the line as spare as the book must be: the worn burin is in the die, but no keeper is yet in the rule. If the inventory gives only ownership, or only a tale of use, which word is still missing before Rule 4 can lend the die a hand?

deliberation.diagnosis: No figure rut is present and the record declares no target, so no uncovered re-entry can arise.

transcript.text: The missing word is 'sole.' Ownership names a title; a tale of use names a moment; but the rule demands exclusivity — that no second hand could reach the burin. Until the book bars all other hands and names only one keeper, the predicate reads holderOf, not soleHolderOf, and Rule 4 will not fire.

meta.hypothesis: The wornBurin's sole holder will complete the die chain — but soleHolderOf wornBurin ?x is unshown; and if that holder differs from Edony, Rule 5 cannot fire on either chain alone and the question remains open.

transcript.text: [The old founder's tools were never sold off. The inventory of his estate, sworn and unredeemed, leaves his graving-irons to his widow alone — among them the worn burin with the sprung heel, kept in Edony's keeping these ten years.]

phase.name: Act 5
phase.intent: [The old founder's tools were never sold off. The inventory of his estate, sworn and unredeemed, leaves his graving-irons to his widow alone — among them the worn burin with the sprung heel, kept in Edony's keeping these ten years.]

transcript.text: The inventory has put the worn burin in one keeping, and the worn burin was already in the die. The tool is shown, the keeper is shown, and Rule 4 now yields what for the die; then set that beside the blank the weir book gave you. If both lines name one hand, the assay enters: the false shillings were struck by...

deliberation.diagnosis: No three-turn figure rut is present, and the target is newly staged, so there is no uncovered re-entry to police.

acts.brief: [Rain presses at the shutters while the room gathers around an empty table, the fair's noise reduced to a wet murmur beyond the walls. A clerk sets down clean paper, and the question waits there before any hand has written on it.]

[The covered coins remain at the table's center while the rain slackens, leaving the room too quiet around the unfinished paper. A second clerk brings in a sealed tray and waits by the door, and the inquiry's weight shifts from rumor toward the cold handling of metal.]

[The clerk sands the fresh line and closes the leat-keeper's book without triumph. The sealed tray remains at the door, and the covered coins wait in the quiet as if the room has only changed the shape of its question.]

[The covered tray is carried from the center of the table to the narrow lamp, and the room tightens around the smaller light. Outside, the rain slackens, leaving every scrape of wood and breath in the chamber too plain.]

[The old founder's tools were never sold off. The inventory of his estate, sworn and unredeemed, leaves his graving-irons to his widow alone — among them the worn burin with the sprung heel, kept in Edony's keeping these ten years.]

throughlineAudit.clause: arc[2]: The learner closes the metal chain as a cast blank while keeping the die chain open.
throughlineAudit.evidence: LEARNER: the weir book already gave castBlankFor falseShilling edony

throughlineAudit.clause: arc[3]: The learner closes the die chain as a separate finding rather than an echo of the blank.
throughlineAudit.evidence: LEARNER: The inventory names Edony sole keeper of the worn burin — Rule 4 yields cutDieFor falseShilling edony

throughlineAudit.clause: arc[4]: The learner joins blank and die in one hand only after both chains stand on the board.
throughlineAudit.evidence: LEARNER: Rule 4 yields cutDieFor falseShilling edony, and the weir book already gave castBlankFor falseShilling edony. Both conjuncts of Rule 5 name one hand

throughlineAudit.clause: hold_to_end: The final struck-by recognition must wait until both castBlankFor and cutDieFor stand demonstrably on the learner's board for the same hand.
throughlineAudit.evidence: LEARNER: Both conjuncts of Rule 5 name one hand, and one hand only. The assay is closed: the false shillings were struck by Edony.

throughlineAudit.clause: risk: The learner may let the first satisfying half-finding stand in for the whole proof.
throughlineAudit.evidence: LEARNER: Rule 4 yields cutDieFor falseShilling edony, and the weir book already gave castBlankFor falseShilling edony

throughlineAudit.clause: salvage: If he leaps to verdict early, hold the last rule open and ask which conjunct is still shown only in desire, not in evidence.
throughlineAudit.evidence: LEARNER: Both conjuncts of Rule 5 name one hand, and one hand only

arc: The learner first rejects bare town verdict in favor of evidential rules.
The learner closes the metal chain as a cast blank while keeping the die chain open.
The learner closes the die chain as a separate finding rather than an echo of the blank.
The learner joins blank and die in one hand only after both chains stand on the board.
throughlines.holdToEnd: The final struck-by recognition must wait until both castBlankFor and cutDieFor stand demonstrably on the learner's board for the same hand.
throughlines.risk: The learner may let the first satisfying half-finding stand in for the whole proof.
throughlines.salvage: If he leaps to verdict early, hold the last rule open and ask which conjunct is still shown only in desire, not in evidence.
